html:
Pär Fabian Lagerkvist, born May 23, 1891, in Växjö, and died July 11, 1974, in Lidingö, was a Swedish author, poet, and playwright. He became a member of the Swedish Academy in 1940 and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1951.

== Biography ==

=== Early Years and Works ===

Pär Lagerkvist was born as the youngest son of Anders Johan Lagerquist (1853–1947), a railway employee, and Johanna Magnusdotter Blad (1854–1923). His childhood home was in the workers' housing near the railway station, located on what later became the railway restaurant grounds, and it was a Pietist household in Växjö. He had six older siblings. As a teacher at the Växjö upper secondary school, he was inspired by the enthusiastic newcomer Josef Fredbärj (1863–1914), who encouraged him to change his name from "Per Lagerquist" to "Pär Lagerkvist." He studied in Uppsala from 1911 to 1912 and spent long periods in Denmark, France, and Italy during the 1910s and 1920s. In his parents' home, as he himself expressed it, the Bible and hymn book were the only books available, but during his teenage years, Lagerkvist came into contact with Darwinism and socialism – new ideas that fundamentally challenged the safe Pietist worldview.

Lagerkvist himself described his background, the intellectual rebellion of his adolescence, and the anxiety and worry it caused in the novel "Guest of Reality" (1925). The collection of short stories "Dark Tales" (1924) includes the story "Father and I," which also has clear autobiographical elements.

He abandoned traditional Christian beliefs during his upper secondary school years in Växjö, but he retained a strong interest in biblical and mythological narratives as literary motifs. Much of his later writing revolved around humanity's place in a world where God no longer reigns, and about God seen as a human projection – to the extent that many perceived him as a Christian against his will. In the poem "A Letter Came," he writes about a letter from his old mother that reminds him of what is eternal. The poem was set to music by Erik Fabiansson and recorded by the church singer Einar Ekberg (on Odeon) in 1938.

His sons, Ulf and Bengt Lagerkvist, have clearly stated that their home was not religious in any way, and that their father never referred privately to a personal God. It is certain that Lagerkvist knew about C. G. Jung's theories on the collective unconscious and on religious, literary, and depth psychological symbolism, but whether he understood them or how much they influenced him remains unclear.

=== Continued Writing === During the 1910s, the author also began writing art criticism. He was one of the first Swedish critics to become acquainted with Picasso's Cubist paintings during a trip to Paris in 1912, and he wrote about these new movements in articles as well as in "Ordkonst och bildkonst." He also wrote several articles on socialist themes during this time, including for the newspaper Politiken.

Lagerkvist's political radicalism persisted throughout his life. Erik Hjalmar Linder has pointed out that the play "The King" (1932) reveals a perspective close to Marxism, and he became an unwavering opponent of Adolf Hitler from 1933 onwards.

Lagerkvist was elected a member of the Swedish Academy in 1940, received an honorary doctorate in philosophy from Gothenburg in 1941, and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1951. He made his debut with the short novel "Människor" (People) in 1912, which he later distanced himself from. He describes the secularized person's inevitable anxiety in the face of a world without God, or, if you will, a belief in a god that no longer exists. This theme recurs in several of his works. Perhaps his most famous poem from the collection "Angst" (Anxiety), 1916, is: "Anxiety, anxiety is my inheritance..." and it stems from this feeling. The theme is also addressed in novels such as "Barabbas," "Sibylla," and "Ahasverus' Death."

Lagerkvist has important connections to existential philosophical currents and is one of the central figures in 20th-century Swedish poetry; despite rarely using pure free verse, and generally maintaining a clearly articulated discursive structure in his poems, his bold concentration in imagery, his closeness to popular and biblical language, and his persistent focus on the subjective brought about innovation, and from this perspective, he is one of the gateway figures of Swedish modernism.

=== Later Works === In "The Executioner" (1933, also a play), the problem of evil is portrayed in a striking way within a medieval setting and then juxtaposed with contemporary brutality. The executioner himself then speaks in a speech that relates humanity's eternal ruthlessness, coldness, longing, and goodness to Christianity. In the first part's primitive medieval tavern environment, the executioner is primarily synonymous with evil. However, in the modern restaurant scene, the satire clearly targets Nazism, but ultimately, the executioner rises above this decline. The book was also one of the writings burned by German Nazis in 1933; in "Der Stürmer," Lagerkvist was called a "judenknecht" (Jewish servant), which, according to his son Ulf, he considered a badge of honor. It provoked a strong reaction in Sweden.

Shortly after "The Executioner," Lagerkvist made a trip...

How certain can we be about this?

People from the 20th century are extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts but rather interpretation and evaluation, which can change over time and with different perspectives.
tldr: Pär Lagerkvist was a highly renowned Swedish author and poet who won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1951. He is known for his writings that explore human emotions and beliefs within a modern context.
lattlast:
Pär Lagerkvist was born in Växjö in 1891 and grew up in a very religious household. As he got older, he began reading new ideas that led him to abandon his previous beliefs, but he continued to use stories from the Bible and ancient myths in his books.

He wrote poetry, novels, and plays. He was an important figure in Swedish literature during the 20th century because he dared to write about difficult subjects such as anxiety and how people live without having a god to rely on. He also became a member of the Swedish Academy, which is a group that selects the recipients of the Nobel Prize.
